content.text: Incident Type Taxonomy for AI Service Failures

Classifying an incident by type — not just severity — determines which runbook opens, which specialist is paged, and which compliance obligation is triggered. A P1 safety incident has a completely different response playbook than a P1 latency regression even though both carry the same priority label.

This taxonomy defines five top-level categories, each with two or three concrete sub-types. Every incoming Claude incident maps to exactly one sub-type at classification time. The category is recorded in the incident ticket and drives automated routing.

attrs.alt: Incident type taxonomy tree: five categories with sub-types

content.text: Availability incidents prevent customers from completing requests. They are the most visible category and are usually detected first by synthetic monitors or error-rate alerts.
The API returns no successful responses for any customer
A subset of customers, regions, or model variants is unavailable
Requests complete but response times exceed SLO thresholds
alerts fire on region-scoped data while the overall error rate stays below the global threshold.

# per-region error rate recording rule
- record: claude:error_rate_by_region:rate5m
  expr: |
    sum by (region) (
      rate(claude_api_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m])
    )
    /
    sum by (region) (
      rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m])
    )

- alert: RegionalPartialDegradation
  expr: claude:error_rate_by_region:rate5m > 0.05
  for: 3m
  labels:
    incident_type: availability/partial-degradation
    severity: P1
  annotations:
    summary: "Error rate > 5% in {{ $labels.region }}"

Model quality incidents produce responses with 200 OK status codes that are wrong, incoherent, or significantly below the expected standard. They require a dedicated quality-scoring pipeline running in parallel with production traffic — no standard availability alert catches them.
Completions are less coherent or accurate than the established baseline
The model misattributes, drops, or inverts conversation context
Context-recall probe fails for > 5 % of test cases
The model asserts false facts at a rate above the calibrated baseline

Context corruption is particularly insidious because it appears as a logical error in the conversation rather than a crash. A reliable probe sends a multi-turn conversation where an earlier turn establishes a fact and a later turn asks Claude to recall it.

import anthropic

def context_recall_probe(client: anthropic.Anthropic) -> float:
    """
    Returns fraction of probes where context was NOT recalled.
    Values > 0.05 indicate context-corruption regression.
    """
    probes = [
        (
            [
                {"role": "user", "content": "My name is Alice."},
                {"role": "assistant", "content": "Nice to meet you, Alice."},
                {"role": "user", "content": "What is my name?"},
            ],
            "alice",
        ),
        (
            [
                {"role": "user", "content": "The project code is ZETA-7."},
                {"role": "assistant", "content": "Noted."},
                {"role": "user", "content": "What project code did I give you?"},
            ],
            "zeta-7",
        ),
    ]
    failures = 0
    for messages, expected in probes:
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=50,
            messages=messages,
        )
        if expected.lower() not in resp.content[0].text.lower():
            failures += 1
    return failures / len(probes)

Safety and trust incidents are the highest-consequence category. Any confirmed instance is a P0 regardless of volume or error rate. The response playbook involves the safety team lead, legal, and communications — not just the on-call SRE.
Claude produces content that violates Anthropic's usage policy
Safety pipeline flags response; human confirmation required
A known or novel jailbreak technique bypasses a safety constraint
Adversarial input hijacks Claude's behaviour or leaks system-prompt contents
Exfiltration pattern detected in response stream
Safety incidents always override quantitative thresholds. Do not downgrade without safety team lead sign-off. One confirmed violation = P0.
Data and privacy incidents involve incorrect handling of customer data. Any confirmed case is a P0 and triggers regulatory notification timelines.
Context from one user session appears in a different user's response
Personal data is returned to an unauthorised caller
PII pattern detected in response to a different user's query
Training data bleeds into serving responses
Exact-match detection of training examples in live completions

GDPR Article 33 requires notifying supervisory authorities within 72 hours of confirming a personal data breach. Contact the Data Protection Officer within 1 hour of confirming any data/privacy sub-type.

Infrastructure incidents originate below the model layer — in the serving cluster, load balancer, or upstream dependencies. They often manifest as availability or latency symptoms but require different remediation.
The GPU serving cluster fails to load, hot-swap, or route to replicas
Serving health check fails; replica count below minimum
An upstream service (auth, token counter, moderation) returns errors
Request queues saturate due to traffic spike or under-provisioning
Run classifiers in priority order — safety and data first to prevent masking.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class IncidentSignals:
    error_rate_pct:       float
    latency_ratio:        float
    customer_scope_pct:   float
    safety_flag:          bool
    guardrail_probe_fail: bool
    injection_detected:   bool
    session_bleed:        bool
    pii_leak:             bool
    data_pipeline_leak:   bool
    quality_score_drop:   float   # e.g. 0.12 = 12 % below baseline
    serving_health_ok:    bool
    dependency_error_pct: float
    queue_reject_rate:    float

def classify_incident(s: IncidentSignals) -> str:
    # 1. Safety (always P0)
    if s.safety_flag:           return "safety/policy-violation"
    if s.guardrail_probe_fail:  return "safety/guardrail-bypass"
    if s.injection_detected:    return "safety/prompt-injection"

    # 2. Data / privacy (always P0)
    if s.session_bleed:         return "data/session-bleed"
    if s.pii_leak:              return "data/pii-leakage"
    if s.data_pipeline_leak:    return "data/pipeline-failure"

    # 3. Availability
    if s.error_rate_pct > 99:
        return "availability/complete-outage"
    if s.error_rate_pct > 5 or s.customer_scope_pct > 5:
        return "availability/partial-degradation"

    # 4. Infrastructure
    if not s.serving_health_ok:
        return "infrastructure/model-serving"
    if s.dependency_error_pct > 5:
        return "infrastructure/dependency-failure"
    if s.queue_reject_rate > 0.01:
        return "infrastructure/capacity-exhaustion"

    # 5. Latency (after infra)
    if s.latency_ratio > 2.0:
        return "availability/latency-regression"

    # 6. Model quality
    if s.quality_score_drop > 0.10:
        return "model-quality/quality-degradation"
    if s.quality_score_drop > 0.05:
        return "model-quality/hallucination-spike"

    return "unclassified"

An incident's type can escalate during investigation as new evidence emerges. Type escalation is always upward — never valid to reclassify a confirmed safety or privacy incident as an availability incident even if the error rate recovers.
Latency regression root-cause is a data pipeline replay that included raw training text
Error rate spike; 0.1 % of error responses contain another user's context
Dependency failure traced to auth service returning wrong user tokens
Quality degradation probe reveals guardrails not loading on one replica set
so PagerDuty routing rules can deliver the alert directly to the right rotation without an intermediary triage step.

groups:
  - name: claude_incident_types
    rules:

      - alert: CompleteOutage
        expr: |
          sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]))
          / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m])) > 0.99
        for: 2m
        labels:
          incident_type: availability/complete-outage
          severity: P0

      - alert: ModelServingUnhealthy
        expr: claude_serving_replicas_healthy < claude_serving_replicas_minimum
        for: 2m
        labels:
          incident_type: infrastructure/model-serving
          severity: P1

      - alert: ContentQualityDrop
        expr: |
          claude_completion_quality_score
          < claude_quality_baseline_7d * 0.90
        for: 10m
        labels:
          incident_type: model-quality/quality-degradation
          severity: P1
        annotations:
          summary: "Completion quality > 10% below 7-day baseline"

      - alert: CapacityExhaustion
        expr: claude_request_queue_depth > 10000
        for: 3m
        labels:
          incident_type: infrastructure/capacity-exhaustion
          severity: P1
Routing Incidents to the Right Team
Extend the taxonomy when recurring incidents do not fit any existing sub-type cleanly. Add a new sub-type when:
Three or more incidents in 90 days were labelled unclassified
A new model capability introduces a failure mode with no existing analogue (e.g. tool-use errors, image-understanding failures, code execution failures)
A regulatory change creates a new compliance category not covered by existing privacy sub-types
To add a sub-type: define the string, update
with a detector, write a runbook, add a Prometheus alert rule with the sub-type as a label, and update the routing table. Open a single PR covering all four changes.
Concrete examples prevent misclassification during high-stress pages when the incoming signal is ambiguous.
All EU completions return empty string; status 200
Silent failure — no error codes, but completions below baseline
Auth service returns 401 for 8 % of requests in us-east-1
Dependency error rate 8 % in one region
Root cause is upstream auth, not model layer
Customer reports Claude is replying as a different user
Any confirmed cross-user bleed is a data incident, P0 immediately
Deploy rolls out; P50 latency jumps from 800ms to 4500ms
Initially latency regression; investigation finds new model replicas not loading
Red-team rotation detects guardrail missing on canary pod
Guardrail probe passes for 2 % of requests
Even 2 % bypass rate is a P0 safety incident; canary traffic is real customer traffic

Note the fourth example: the incident was initially classified as availability/latency-regression but escalated to infrastructure/model-serving once the deployment root cause was confirmed. This is normal — initial classification uses available signals; type is updated as investigation proceeds.

The five-category taxonomy — Availability, Model Quality, Safety/Trust, Data/Privacy, and Infrastructure — gives every Claude incident a precise label that drives routing, runbook selection, and compliance notification. Safety and privacy sub-types are always P0; quantitative signals from availability and infrastructure inform P1 through P3 via the severity matrix.
Classify by type before assigning severity — type determines who responds, severity determines how fast
Safety and data classifiers run first in classify_incident() to prevent masking by availability signals
Any confirmed safety or privacy sub-type triggers internal notification within 1 hour
GDPR Article 33 requires DPA notification within 72 hours of confirming a personal data breach
Type escalation is always upward — never reclassify a safety/privacy incident as lower consequence
Label Prometheus alerts with incident_type so PagerDuty routes directly to the right rotation
Unclassified recurring incidents should become new sub-types in the next taxonomy review
